1. The white border moved to (23,41) - this was the orange object at the junction

So clicking on the orange junction object moved it and removed (9,49). This is a different behavior than clicking on trail segments.
Let me think about this. The pattern seems to be:
- Clicking on trail segments moves the white border along the trail
- Clicking on bordered objects (like (42,40) and (23,41)) moves the white border to that object and removes another object
So the goal might be to click on specific objects to remove all the gray-bordered objects.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check adjacency of orange object at (28,38)
seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Find orange object
orange_node = None
for i, n in enumerate(seg['nodes']):
    if n['color'] == 'O' and n['pixels'] > 5:
        orange_node = i
        cr = sum(b[0] for b in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        cc = sum(b[1] for b in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"Orange object at ({cr},{cc}), node_id={i}")

# Check its adjacency
if orange_node is not None:
    print("\nAdjacency of orange object:")
    for edge in seg['adjacency_list']:
        if edge[0] == orange_node or edge[1] == orange_node:
            other = edge[1] if edge[0] == orange_node else edge[0]
            n = seg['nodes'][other]
            cr = sum(b[0] for b in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
            cc = sum(b[1] for b in n['boundary']) // len(n['boundary'])
            print(f"  <-> ({cr},{cc}) color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
